html:
Stoning to death for adultery — and a "lost" verse of the Quran that commanded it Violence Women Contradiction Strong Muslim 4284
"'Umar b. Khattab sat on the pulpit of Allah's Messenger... Verily Allah sent Muhammad with truth and He sent down the Book upon him, and the verse of stoning was included in what was sent down to him. We recited it, retained it in our memory and understood it. Allah's Messenger awarded the punishment of stoning to death (to the married adulterer and adulteress) and, after him, we also awarded the punishment of stoning... Stoning is a duty laid down in Allah's Book..." (4194)

What the hadith says

Two points: the prescribed punishment for married adulterers is death by stoning — not just the 100 lashes in Quran 24:2. And the second caliph ʿUmar publicly declared from the pulpit that a "verse of stoning" was part of the Quran, was recited by the Companions, but is no longer in the current text.

Why this is a problem

  1. It contradicts the Quran. Quran 24:2 prescribes 100 lashes for fornication with no distinction by marital status. The hadith adds stoning for the married — a penalty the Quran nowhere legislates. Classical jurists reconcile the two by adding an unstated qualifier to the Quranic verse derived entirely from hadith, not from the Quran itself.
  2. It admits the Quran is incomplete. ʿUmar's declaration — preserved as authentic — says a verse of Allah was lost from the text. This undermines Quran 15:9 ("We will be its guardian"). If Allah's guardianship allowed a verse with an active legal ruling to vanish, the preservation promise has failed.
  3. It establishes that Islamic law rests on extra-Quranic sources. Every Sharia system that applies stoning does so on the authority of this hadith, not the Quran. This confirms that "the Quran is sufficient" is not a position the classical tradition actually holds.

The Muslim response

Classical scholars developed naskh al-tilawa duna al-hukm — "abrogation of the wording while the ruling remains." Allah deliberately removed the verse from the Quran while keeping its law in force. This concedes that the Quran as we have it is missing revelation, and asks the believer to accept that Allah wanted the text incomplete.

Why it fails

The naskh al-tilawa doctrine was built specifically to absorb embarrassments of this shape. It fails on its own terms: it concedes the present Quran is missing revelation while asserting the Quran is divinely preserved (15:9), and asks the believer to accept that Allah deliberately removed wording while keeping its ruling. The simplest hypothesis — the verse existed and did not survive compilation — is rejected because it breaks preservation theology. Every alternative costs the tradition more than the simple reading would.

The woman from Ghamid — stoned to death after breastfeeding her baby Violence Women Strong Muslim 4302
"There came to him a woman from Ghamid and said: Allah's Messenger, I have committed adultery, so purify me. He turned her away... She said: By Allah, I have become pregnant. He said: Well, if you insist upon it, then go away until you give birth to (the child). When she was delivered she came with the child... He said: Go away and suckle him until you wean him. When she had weaned him, she came... She was put in a ditch up to her chest and he commanded people and they stoned her. Khalid b. Walid came forward with a stone which he flung at her head and there spurted blood on the face of Khalid..."

What the hadith says

A woman confesses adultery. Muhammad sends her away — first to deliver, then to wean her child. She returns a third time with a weaned toddler. She is placed in a pit up to her chest and stoned to death, the Prophet present and commanding. Khalid ibn al-Walid is splattered with her blood.

Why this is a problem

This is a ritualised execution of a woman who repeatedly sought mercy. She confessed four times — the minimum for the hadd — and was sent away each time. The sustained opportunity to let the matter drop was declined. The execution proceeds with explicit Prophetic authority.

Several layers compound the horror: she is partially buried before the stoning — a technique designed to prolong the killing and prevent escape. Khalid curses her after being splashed with blood; Muhammad rebukes him not for participating in the stoning but for cursing her. The narrative closes with Muhammad praising her repentance: the theology is that the execution was the repentance. This hadith is the classical juristic foundation for stoning in Sharia systems. Every modern judicial stoning — Iran, Saudi Arabia, Sudan, northern Nigeria — ultimately traces its authority here.

The Muslim response

"The Prophet gave her every chance to withdraw; her death was her own choice." The hadith preserves Muhammad's reluctance and the procedural delays as evidence of mercy within the law's structure.

Why it fails

The "choice" framing treats death by stoning as proportionate to consensual sex — a moral judgment no modern legal system accepts. It presumes the legitimacy of the penalty under review rather than defending it. "Stoning is mercy compared to hellfire" is a spiritual rationalisation that only works if one already accepts the eschatological premise; from outside that premise, the act is execution for a private moral failing. The procedural delays and the tender pastoral memory of a weaned child do not change the moral status of the outcome.

"I have been commanded to fight against people until they testify there is no god but Allah" Treatment of Disbelievers Violence Strong Muslim 33
"I have been commanded to fight against people, till they testify to the fact that there is no god but Allah, and believe in me (that) I am the messenger (from the Lord) and in all that I have brought. And when they do it, their blood and riches are guaranteed protection on my behalf except where it is justified by law..." (0031)

What the hadith says

Muhammad states that his commission is to fight (uqatila — armed combat) against "the people" until they accept Islam. Only upon conversion are their lives and property protected.

Why this is a problem

This is the foundational hadith for the classical doctrine that warfare against non-Muslims continues until they convert, pay the jizya, or are killed or enslaved. It inverts the ordinary framing in which war requires justification: here, the default state between Muslims and others is war; peace is the exception secured by conversion.

The hadith is cited explicitly in classical Islamic international law (siyar) by al-Shaybani, al-Shafi'i, and al-Mawardi to justify expansionist jihad. It was the theological backbone of the early Islamic conquests (632–750 CE). Modern apologists argue the hadith means "fight those who fight you until they submit," but the Arabic — "an uqatila al-nas hatta" — says "that I fight the people until" with no qualifier. The condition for stopping is not their cessation of hostility; it is their conversion.

The Muslim response

"The 'people' means specifically the polytheists of Arabia" — a narrowing to a historically specific group. Or: "This was context-specific to Muhammad's lifetime and is not a standing command for all Muslims."

Why it fails

The hadith does not say that, and the classical jurists did not read it that way — they applied it to all non-Muslims outside Dar al-Islam. The narrowing is a modern reformist move, not classical doctrine. And if the commission terminated with Muhammad's death, no Islamic school accepts that — the hadith is preserved precisely because it was understood as a general rule, not a one-off contextual instruction.

Mut'ah — permitted, then forbidden, then disputed: temporary marriage on military expeditions Sexual Misconduct Contradiction Abrogation Women Strong Muslim 3288
"We were on an expedition with Allah's Messenger and we had no women with us. We said: Should we not have ourselves castrated? He forbade us to do so. He then granted us permission that we should contract temporary marriage for a stipulated period giving her a garment..." (3243)
"Allah's Messenger said: O people, I had permitted you to contract temporary marriage with women, but Allah has forbidden it (now) until the Day of Resurrection..." (3255)

What the hadith says

Mut'ah ("enjoyment") was a time-limited marriage contracted for days or weeks in exchange for a payment. Companions on military expeditions — separated from their wives — received permission to enter these contracts with Arab women they encountered. After a fixed period, the contracts expired and the men moved on. Distinct hadith groups in Sahih Muslim show Muhammad permitting mut'ah, then forbidding it "until the Day of Resurrection," and Companions including Jabir and Ibn ʿAbbas continuing the practice "during the lifetime of the Holy Prophet and during the time of Abu Bakr and ʿUmar" until ʿUmar banned it.

Why this is a problem

  1. It is, functionally, prostitution with religious sanction. A man pays a woman a garment or dates to have sex with her for three nights. The contract has no continuing obligations. The man is often already married. Modern Muslims would recognise this arrangement outside Islam as prostitution.
  2. The Prophet's own position is unclear. Was mut'ah permanently forbidden by Muhammad, or only by ʿUmar? Shia Muslims (relying on Jabir/Ibn ʿAbbas) hold it is still lawful. Sunni Muslims (relying on the Sabra al-Juhanni line) hold Muhammad banned it. Both sides cite Sahih Muslim.
  3. The abrogation is textually invisible. The Quran does not forbid mut'ah. Some scholars argue 4:24 authorises it. If the Prophet forbade it, the prohibition exists only in hadith — a method of abrogation the Quran does not describe.

The Muslim response

The Sunni defense: mut'ah was a concession during specific campaigns, later revoked by Muhammad himself. The Shia defense: the revocation hadith is fabricated. The argument has lasted 1,400 years precisely because the hadith record is contradictory.

Why it fails

Both sides cannot be right — and a corpus presented as preserved divine authority should not leave a basic sexual-law question this contested. The hadith record contains both permission and revocation, each attested in sahih material. Either the authentication system produces contradictory output — in which case it cannot ground binding law — or one side has been transmitting falsehood as sahih for fourteen centuries. Neither option leaves the doctrine of preserved Sunna intact.

'Azl with captive women — Muhammad permits sex with married women taken in raids Sexual Misconduct Violence Prophetic Character Women Strong Muslim 3421
"We went out with Allah's Messenger on the expedition to the Bi'l-Mustaliq and took captive some excellent Arab women; and we desired them, for we were suffering from the absence of our wives, (but at the same time) we also desired ransom for them. So we decided to have sexual intercourse with them but by observing 'azl... But we said: We are doing an act whereas Allah's Messenger is amongst us; why not ask him? So we asked Allah's Messenger, and he said: It does not matter if you do not do it, for every soul that is to be born up to the Day of Resurrection will be born." (3371)

What the hadith says

Two connected incidents: at the Banu Mustaliq raid, Companions take women captive, intend to ransom them but also want sex in the meantime, and ask whether they may use withdrawal to avoid pregnancy. Muhammad says withdrawal makes no difference — he does not forbid the sex. At Awtas, fighters hesitate because the captive women have living husbands; Quran 4:24 is revealed to clarify that captives are exempt from the "already married" prohibition.

Why this is a problem

By any modern legal and ethical standard, this is rape: the women were not willing participants; they had been captured in battle, their kin killed or captured, their homes overrun. Most had living husbands. The captors' motivation is stated plainly: "we desired them, for we were suffering from the absence of our wives." The women are simultaneously held for ransom and sexually used.

Muhammad's ruling is that there is no moral or legal objection — only a pragmatic issue (withdrawal to preserve ransom value), which he declares irrelevant. The 4:24 revelation is even more striking: when Companions hesitate because these women have husbands, a Quranic verse is revealed to override that hesitation. The problem of married women being raped by conquerors is solved by declaring the marriages annulled upon capture.

The Muslim response

"Islam reformed slavery; this was merciful compared to pre-Islamic norms." Incremental improvement over 7th-century norms is not a moral defense in the 21st century. The defense concedes the descriptive claim: Islam permits sexual intercourse with captive women taken in war.

Why it fails

"The captive became a slave, and slave-concubinage was lawful." Precisely — which is the objection. An ethical system that converts the rape of war captives into a lawful domestic arrangement by the device of enslavement is describing the same act with a different label. The legal category does not change the moral content.

The assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf — Muhammad ordered a murder by deception Violence Prophetic Character Antisemitism Strong Muslim 4533
"The Messenger of Allah said: Who will kill Ka'b b. Ashraf? He has maligned Allah, the Exalted, and His Messenger. Muhammad b. Maslama said: Messenger of Allah, do you wish that I should kill him? He said: Yes. He said: Permit me to talk (to him in the way I deem fit). He said: Talk (as you like)... they killed him."

What the hadith says

Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf was a Jewish poet in Medina who composed verses critical of Muhammad after the Battle of Badr. Muhammad asked "Who will kill Ka'b?" Muhammad b. Maslama volunteered, requesting permission to deceive Ka'b — which Muhammad explicitly granted. The assassins went at night, lured Ka'b out by pretending to want a loan, complimented his perfume, got him to lower his guard, then held his head and killed him.

Why this is a problem

  1. The target was a civilian killed for poetry. Ka'b was not a combatant. His offense was satirical verse and possibly incitement — conducted from his home. The killing took place at night by deception.
  2. Deception was explicitly sanctioned. Muhammad b. Maslama asked for permission to lie; Muhammad's answer was "Talk as you like." This became the foundational precedent for war-deception in Islamic law.
  3. The assassins exploited hospitality. Ka'b, trusting the night-visit, came out unarmed. Arab hospitality norms were weaponised against him.
  4. The modern parallels are direct. When Charlie Hebdo cartoonists were murdered in 2015, killers cited the Ka'b precedent. The same logic animated the assassination of Theo van Gogh and the fatwa against Salman Rushdie. Whenever mainstream Muslim authorities have condemned such killings, they have had to do so against the grain of this hadith.

The Muslim response

"Ka'b had broken a treaty and was actively inciting war against Medina." This is the strongest defense — Ibn Ishaq describes Ka'b travelling to Mecca to urge the Quraysh to avenge Badr. His actions went beyond poetry to active military incitement.

Why it fails

A lawful response to treaty violation is open warfare or expulsion, not targeted assassination by deception. The Prophet did not summon Ka'b to answer charges; he authorised a murder squad. "Poetry was a weapon in 7th-century Arabia" is true as cultural context, but the principle that verbal offense justifies extrajudicial killing has been Islam's export ever since — applied to novelists, cartoonists, and filmmakers in the 21st century.

"They are from them" — Muhammad permits killing of polytheist women and children in night raids Violence Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Muslim 4417
"Sa'b b. Jaththama (the Prophet of Allah was) asked about the women and children of the polytheists being killed during the night raid, said: They are from them." (4321)
"What about the children of polytheists killed by the cavalry during the night raid? He said: They are from them." (4323)

What the hadith says

In a night raid, attackers cannot easily distinguish combatants from women and children. The Companions ask Muhammad whether killing them is permissible. His answer — preserved in three separate variants — is "they are from them": the children of polytheists share their parents' status and may be killed collaterally.

Why this is a problem

This hadith directly contradicts the immediately-preceding chapter of Sahih Muslim, which records Muhammad forbidding the killing of women and children (Muslim 4415). The classical resolution: women and children cannot be deliberately targeted, but may be killed as collateral damage in night raids because they cannot be distinguished from combatants.

The "they are from them" formulation — hum minhum — is cited by ISIS, al-Qaeda, and Boko Haram to justify attacks where civilian casualties are certain. A doctrine that kills children because of their parents' religion assigns guilt by inheritance. Each Quranic passage about individual accountability (35:18, 53:38) sits in tension with this hadith. The ruling is preserved as general settled law across three transmission variants — not a one-off contextual answer.

The Muslim response

"The Prophet allowed this only when civilians could not be distinguished — not as a license to target children." The distinction between collateral and deliberate killing is real, and classical jurists attempted to restrict application. The hadith addresses a specific tactical problem, not a general permission to kill non-combatants.

Why it fails

"Civilians could not be distinguished" has no operational content when the attacker is the one judging distinguishability. Every jihadist group that has cited this hadith has claimed it applied to their attacks, and the text offers no procedural check on that claim. The restrictions the apologist invokes live entirely in later juristic commentary, not in the hadith itself. A theological blanket that needs downstream jurists to write the conditions under which it won't apply is not a rule restricting the killing of children; it is a rule permitting it with deniable qualifications.

The Banu Qurayza massacre — "kill their fighters and capture their women and children" Violence Antisemitism Prophetic Character Strong Muslim 4464
"The people of Quraiza surrendered accepting the decision of Sa'd b. Mu'adh about them... He (Sa'd) said: You will kill their fighters and capture their women and children. (Hearing this), the Prophet said: You have adjudged by the command of God." (4368)

What the hadith says

After the Battle of the Trench (627 CE), the Jewish tribe of Banu Qurayza surrendered and accepted the arbitration of Saʿd ibn Muʿadh. His verdict: kill the fighting-age men; enslave the women and children. Muhammad ratified this as "the command of God." Classical sources record approximately 600–900 Jewish men executed in a single day — beheaded one by one. Women and children were distributed as slaves.

Why this is a problem

  1. Muhammad validated the verdict as divine. "You have adjudged by the command of God" removes any possibility this was passive tolerance. He explicitly endorsed it as religious law.
  2. The verdict was delivered after surrender. The Qurayza were not killed in combat; they were executed as defeated captives who had accepted arbitration.
  3. The rationale was collective. Even accepting the accusation of treaty-breaking, collective punishment of all adult males for the acts of leadership has no defensible moral framework.
  4. The children of the executed became slaves of the executioners. This is not a contested account — it appears in both Bukhari and Muslim, and in every major biographical source.

The Muslim response

"The Qurayza had betrayed a treaty during a siege; they posed an existential threat to Medina." Accepted as a causal account of why the conflict occurred.

Why it fails

Treaty violation by leaders does not justify the mass execution of surrendered prisoners — this fails on both 7th-century honour-war norms and modern international law. "This was ordinary Arabian warfare" does not work either: Islam claims to bring moral universalism, not merely to adapt to local custom. If Islamic ethics are indexed to 7th-century Arabian norms, they are not universal. And "Saʿd made the verdict, not Muhammad" fails because Muhammad explicitly blessed it as the command of God — the hadith records his active endorsement, not passive acquiescence.

"I will expel the Jews and Christians from the Arabian Peninsula" Treatment of Disbelievers Antisemitism Strong Muslim 4462
"Umar b. al-Khattab heard the Messenger of Allah say: I will expel the Jews and Christians from the Arabian Peninsula and will not leave any but Muslim."

What the hadith says

Muhammad declares the Arabian peninsula must be religiously monoreligious — Jews and Christians are to be expelled; only Muslims may remain. Implemented under ʿUmar, who expelled the Jews of Khaybar and the Christians of Najran. It remains in force today: Saudi Arabia bars non-Muslim worship; Mecca and Medina are closed to non-Muslims altogether.

Why this is a problem

This is religious ethnic cleansing prescribed as Prophetic policy. The hadith supplies the theological basis for Saudi Arabia's current non-Muslim restrictions — a live, currently-implemented policy. It sits in tension with Quranic verses frequently cited to defend Islamic tolerance: "There is no compulsion in religion" (2:256) is voided within Arabia itself; "To you be your religion, and to me mine" (109:6) is rendered inapplicable in the Prophet's own homeland. Mainstream Sunni law has resolved the tension by restricting pluralism to non-Arabian territories and enforcing the expulsion principle in the Hijaz — revealing the pluralism to be contingent on geography, not principle.

The Muslim response

"The Arabian peninsula is a sacred space — the expulsion was spatial purification, not religious hostility." The analogy is to restricted Temple zones in ancient Judaism. The two cities' sacred status makes them a special case rather than a general principle of religious segregation.

Why it fails

Relabelling the expulsion as "spatial purification" rather than "religious hostility" is a semantic move, not a defense. The effect is identical: entire religious communities are removed from a territory solely because of their religion. The apologist's own observation — no equivalent Christian or Jewish doctrine exists for Jerusalem or Rome — is telling: Islam alone among the Abrahamic traditions theologises a sacred-land clearance of other faiths. Modern enforcement under Saudi Arabia shows the doctrine is not a historical curiosity.

The gharqad hadith — at the last hour, stones and trees will identify Jews for Muslims to kill Eschatology Antisemitism Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Muslim 7158
"Abu Huraira reported Allah's Messenger as saying: The last hour would not come unless the Muslims will fight against the Jews and the Muslims would kill them until the Jews would hide themselves behind a stone or a tree and a stone or a tree would say: Muslim, or the servant of Allah, there is a Jew behind me; come and kill him; but the tree Gharqad would not say, for it is the tree of the Jews."

What the hadith says

The end of the world comes only after a final war in which Muslims hunt and kill Jews — with stones and trees miraculously crying out to reveal Jewish hiding places. The gharqad tree alone will remain silent, because it is "the tree of the Jews."

Why this is a problem

This is a hadith of apocalyptic genocide. It imagines the end of history as the successful extermination of the Jewish people by Muslims, with the natural world enlisted as accessory. It is preserved in Sahih Muslim — the second-most authoritative collection — narrated by Abu Huraira, and cited in Article 7 of the 1988 Hamas Covenant as theological justification for war against Israel. It is a staple of modern Islamist antisemitic preaching. The gharqad exception — "the tree of the Jews" — makes clear the referent is Jewish ethno-religious identity, not a subset allied with a specific enemy. The text says "the Jews," not "some Jews."

The Muslim response

"This is eschatological prophecy, not a command to act in the present." The hadith describes what will happen at the end of time, not what Muslims should do now. The "Jews" of the final battle are followers of the Dajjal specifically — a supernatural antichrist figure — not Jewish communities as a whole.

Why it fails

The prophecy has functioned for 1,400 years as a background assumption shaping Muslim-Jewish relations. Hamas's founding charter cites it directly as a mandate for killing Jews — not as distant eschatology. Israeli far-right groups plant Gharqad trees specifically in response to the hadith's prophecy. A scripture-status text that functions as prophetic warrant for genocide in the 21st century is not neutralised by claiming its application was meant to be restricted to the end of time. The eschatology is operational now — which is exactly the problem.

The Night Journey and Ascent — Buraq, seven heavens, and bargaining with Moses over prayers Prophetic Character Strange / Obscure Strong Muslim 316
"I was brought al-Buraq Who is an animal white and long, larger than a donkey but smaller than a mule, who would place his hoof a distance equal to the range of vision. I mounted it and came to the Temple... Then he took me to heaven... I went back to my Lord and said: My Lord, make things lighter for my Ummah. (The Lord) reduced five prayers for me. I went down to Moses and said. (The Lord) reduced five (prayers) for me, He said: Verily thy Ummah shall not be able to bear this burden; return to thy Lord and ask Him to make things lighter..." (Muslim 316)

What the hadith says

Muhammad rides Buraq from Mecca to Jerusalem, then ascends through seven heavens meeting previous prophets at each level. At the top he receives the command for 50 daily prayers. Descending, Moses advises him to negotiate a reduction. Muhammad returns repeatedly to Allah, each time reducing by five, until settling at five daily prayers.

Why this is a problem

  1. The Buraq is specified in physical detail. An animal larger than a donkey, smaller than a mule, each stride reaching the horizon. This is folklore-level specificity for an entity not mentioned in the Quran.
  2. The bargain with Moses has three theological problems. It depicts Allah as negotiable; it depicts Moses as more realistic about human capacity than both Allah and Muhammad; and it implies the final ruling (five prayers) was not Allah's first choice — Moses talked Him down from fifty.
  3. The heart-washing passage (#0311). Gabriel tears open Muhammad's breast as a child, extracts "the part of Satan" from his heart, washes it in Zamzam water, and returns it. This is legendary material presented as history.
  4. The cosmological picture — layered physical heavens with seated prophets — does not correspond to any observable structure.

The Muslim response

"Some of these details are metaphorical." That is the modern rescue — the Buraq is symbolic, the heavens are spiritual states, the negotiation is a pedagogical device about human limitation.

Why it fails

Classical Sunni tradition — al-Tabari, Ibn Kathir, al-Qurtubi — read the account literally: a physical Buraq, physical layered heavens, a physical negotiation. The "metaphorical" move is a 20th-century response to the narrative's obvious strain under modern cosmology. It concedes the point: the tradition cannot defend the plain text against scientific knowledge, so it retreats to allegory. But allegory was not the canonical reading for 1,200 years.

The majority of hell's inhabitants are women Women Strong Muslim 6767
"I stood at the door of Paradise and I found that the overwhelming majority of those who entered therein was that of poor persons... The denizens of Hell were commanded to get into Hell, and I stood upon the door of Fire and the majority amongst them who entered there was that of women." (6596)
"Amongst the inmates of Paradise the women would form a minority." (6600)

What the hadith says

Muhammad reports, as if from direct vision, that most inhabitants of hell are women and women form a minority in paradise. Multiple independent chains of transmission preserve this claim.

Why this is a problem

  1. It is an empirical claim about disproportionate moral failure by sex. Not "women face different trials" — "women predominate in hell." Parallel hadith material (Bukhari 304, Muslim 132) specifies the reasons: women curse frequently and are ungrateful to their husbands.
  2. It is paired with a "women as trial" theme. Immediately following hadiths (6603–6606) describe women as "the greatest turmoil for men" — not the greatest test, but the primary cause of damnation in men. The compilation is coherent: women are both the majority of the damned and the primary cause of damnation in men.
  3. Modern Muslim women face an impossible choice. Either accept a framework that predicts their statistical overrepresentation in hellfire based on sex, or reject the hadith — which undermines the hadith science apparatus that grounds Sunni Islam.

The Muslim response

"Women appear more often because they are more emotional / curse more / complain about husbands." This is the classical apologetic — it explains the disparity by assigning women moral deficits, which is exactly the claim under objection.

Why it fails

"The hadith reflects 7th-century Arabian gender dynamics, not universal truth." Then the hadith is not divine revelation; it is cultural artifact. That move is available, but it surrenders the traditional epistemology: either the Prophet's sahih reports tell us about reality, or they don't. Selectively demoting the uncomfortable ones to cultural artifact while preserving the others is ad hoc — and the tradition has not applied this filter consistently.
